model_summary: Select all six complaint-derived criteria (health-focused worry, catastrophic symptom interpretation, compulsive internet checking, failure to inhibit checking, perceived loss of control, acute panic on symptom onset) and seven modifiable, high-signal predictors drawn from the PSYCHO domain: cognitive restructuring/probabilistic thinking (P01), scheduled checking & digital-hygiene (P02), interoceptive exposure (P03), mindfulness/acceptance & distress-tolerance (P04), executive-inhibition/urge-control training (P05), sleep/circadian stabilization (P06), and social support/accountability (P07). Measurement uses intensive EMA (6 prompts/day, 28 days) for momentary state and skill-practice adherence, a single morning diary for sleep anchoring, and optional passive phone streams (per-prompt aggregation of screen minutes and unlock counts). The design prioritizes within-person variance, minimal collinearity (one broad self-regulation/inhibition predictor), clear recall windows, and a feasible observation count for later gVAR estimation (≈168 momentary observations + 28 morning diaries).
variable_selection_notes: All six complaint-derived criteria (C01–C06) are retained to preserve the complaint's full resolution and to enable multivariate outcome modeling. Predictors were chosen to (1) map directly to high-ranked HyDe / LLM solution nodes (cognitive restructuring, metacognitive/attention training, exposure/urge-work, digital hygiene, inhibitory control, sleep, social accountability), (2) be modifiable by brief interventions or behavioral routines, and (3) have realistic EMA or passive measures with expected within-person variability. Trade-offs: many HyDe nodes are closely related (e.g., various CBT skill families); to reduce collinearity we merged overlapping concepts into single, broader predictors (e.g., P01 covers cognitive restructuring and probabilistic thinking; P04 covers mindfulness/acceptance/distress tolerance). Passive measures (screen minutes/unlocks) are included but optional (consent-dependent) because they increase objectivity for checking behavior but risk missingness. Uncertainty/bias notes: self-report of checking duration may be biased; passive phone signals are a proxy (not content-specific) and may miss non-phone checking. We prioritized constructs most directly tied to the complaint's mechanisms (catastrophizing, threat monitoring, reassurance-seeking/compulsivity, urge-inhibition, arousal) to keep the model estimable for gVAR.

momentary_ema.notes: Six prompts/day balances temporal resolution (0-6h lag sensitivity) and participant burden. Blocks include jitter to avoid predictability. EMA items below are asked at each prompt unless otherwise noted.

daily_diary.notes: Morning diary captures prior-night sleep timing and quality for P06 (sleep/circadian stabilization).

passive_streams.notes: If participant consents, collect phone OS aggregated screen_time_minutes and screen_unlock_count; aggregate into the same recall window as EMA (since last prompt). Passive data increases objectivity for checking behavior but may be unavailable for some participants (iOS/Android privacy limits).

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Expected ~168 momentary observations + 28 daily diaries per participant (if adherence high). This should be sufficient for gVAR with 13 time-series (6 criteria + 7 predictors) after variable selection/reduction; initial gVAR may require pruning to the strongest predictors or dimension reduction if effective sample drops below targets.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Use 1–9 anchored Likert for subjective items to maximize within-person variance. For duration-oriented items (checking) record self-reported minutes (continuous) and an intensity/urge 1–9 scale to avoid rare-event issues.
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Main risks: passive stream opt-out, skipped EMA prompts, and missing passive windows (OS restrictions). Recommend push reminders, brief items (<30s), and incentives for adherence. Plan for multiple imputation and time-series appropriate missing-data methods; include per-prompt passive-availability flags.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how much have you worried about your health? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Extremely)
measurement.notes: Captures momentary fluctuation in worry; asked each prompt.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering and z-scoring per participant; consider detrending if systematic drift

criteria_variables.label: Catastrophic interpretation of minor bodily symptoms

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, if you noticed bodily sensations, to what extent did you interpret them as indicating something very serious (e.g., convinced you might be dying)? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Completely)
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: 1-9 Likert (anchored) plus conditional event flag if no sensation
measurement.notes: If no sensations occurred, record 1 on intensity and set event_flag=false. This measures appraisal intensity rather than trait belief.

measurement.channel: smartphone EMA app + optional passive phone sensor
measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you search for health information online? If yes, how many minutes did you spend? (enter minutes). Also rate how strong the urge was on a 1-9 scale (1 = No urge, 9 = Very strong urge).
measurement.notes: If passive consent is given, also collect screen_time_minutes_browser_since_last_prompt and screen_unlock_count as passive proxies to cross-validate self-report.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: log1p(minutes) for skewed duration; person-mean center urge ratings

criteria_variables.label: Failure to inhibit checking despite recognizing irrationality

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you try and fail to resist checking/searching even though you knew it was irrational? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Completely)
measurement.notes: Captures repeated engagement and perceived failure to inhibit; complements objective duration measures.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering; consider binary flag for any failure events if distribution highly zero-inflated

criteria_variables.label: Subjective sense of loss of control and distress about it

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how much did you feel that your health worries were out of control and how distressed did that make you? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Extremely)
measurement.notes: Captures perceived control and associated distress; central to user's complaint.

criteria_variables.label: Acute panic-level fear when a symptom appears

measurement.item_or_signal: If you noticed a bodily symptom since the last prompt, how intense was your immediate fear/panic? (1 = Not at all, 9 = Extreme panic). Also mark event_flag if a symptom occurred.
measurement.notes: Event-anchored intensity is recorded; if no symptom, intensity = 1 and event_flag=false. This supports modeling of cue-triggered panic events.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering; consider event-based models for event_flag-conditioned analyses

predictor_variables.label: Cognitive restructuring / probabilistic thinking practice
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Teaches reappraisal and probabilistic estimates to reduce catastrophic interpretations and global health worry; directly targets appraisal processes.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Brief guided reappraisal exercises (identify thought, evidence for/against, alternative explanations; probability estimation exercises).

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you use a cognitive reappraisal/probability-checking strategy? If yes, rate how effective it was (1=Not at all,9=Very effective) and record minutes spent practicing.
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: binary + 1-9 Likert for effectiveness + minutes practiced
measurement.notes: Practice frequency and perceived effectiveness will be used as time-varying predictors; minutes practice often low but effective for short-term shifts.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: use minutes (log1p) and effectiveness (person-mean center); binary practice flag as additional covariate

expected_effects.rationale: Reappraisal reduces momentary worry; effects can accumulate over hours.
Directly targets catastrophic interpretation schemas.
Reducing threat appraisals should reduce checking driven by reassurance-seeking, though effect may be indirect and slower.
Increased cognitive control over appraisals supports resisting checks.
Improved appraisals increase perceived influence and reduce distress about loss of control.
Lower catastrophic appraisals can reduce panic intensity when symptoms occur.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Low risk; ensure exercises do not increase avoidance. If exposure is used in conjunction, supervise.

feasibility.notes: Self-report practice and minutes are easy to collect; expected to vary within-person across days.

predictor_variables.label: Scheduled checking & digital hygiene (if-then plans and limits)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Creates rules and boundaries for information-search behavior, reducing compulsive, unstructured checking and long search sessions.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Set specific 20-minute scheduled windows for health information seeking, use website blockers, implement 'urge-if/then' plans.

measurement.channel: smartphone EMA app + optional passive phone sensor
measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you check health information outside scheduled windows? (Y/N). Self-reported minutes spent searching and perceived adherence to schedule on 1-9 scale. Passive proxy: browser_time_minutes_since_last_prompt and screen_unlock_count.
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: binary + minutes + 1-9 adherence Likert; passive minutes if available
measurement.notes: Pairs with passive streams to capture objective phone activity; ideal for modeling immediate antecedents of checking episodes.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: log1p(minutes) for durations; use adherence Likert person-mean centered

expected_effects.rationale: Reduced uncontrolled searching reduces maintenance of worry over the day.
Sometimes brief checking reduces uncertainty; excessive unstructured checking increases catastrophic interpretations — effect may be nonlinear.
Directly targets checking duration; close temporal effects expected.
Scheduled checking and if-then plans increase capacity to inhibit checking impulses.
Regaining behavioral control should increase perceived control over time.
Indirect effect via reduced reassurance-seeking; timing uncertain.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: If implementing website blockers, ensure user can access health services in case of real concerns.

feasibility.notes: Passive phone metrics improve measurement but require consent and may be unavailable on some devices.

predictor_variables.label: Interoceptive exposure to feared bodily sensations
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Repeated planned exposure to benign bodily sensations reduces catastrophic conditioned fear and panic reactivity via inhibitory learning.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Guided low-intensity interoceptive exercises (light hyperventilation-like exercises, spinning head movements in low-intensity controlled setting) with grounding and safety plan.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you practice interoceptive exposure exercises? (Y/N). If yes, minutes practiced and peak fear during exercise (1-9).
measurement.notes: Exposure sessions may be infrequent initially; capture both practice and intensity to model habituation effects.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: minutes: log1p; peak fear: person-mean centering

expected_effects.rationale: Repeated exposure reduces baseline worry over days via extinction/inhibitory learning.
Targets catastrophic associations directly; reductions expected after sessions.
Less fear-driven checking expected as interoceptive tolerance improves.
Greater tolerance supports resisting checking impulses.
Mastery of bodily tolerance increases perceived control over time.
Primarily reduces acute panic reactivity to bodily cues.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Exposure can transiently increase arousal; provide safety instructions and stop rules. Avoid high-intensity exercises without clinician support.

feasibility.notes: Practice sessions may be sparse; consider modeling session-level effects with multi-timescale lags.

predictor_variables.label: Mindfulness/acceptance & distress-tolerance skills
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Reduces reactivity and fused identification with worry and bodily sensations; increases urge-surfing capacity.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Brief mindful body scans, urge-surfing instructions, acceptance prompts, and paced breathing practices.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you use a mindfulness/acceptance or breathing exercise? If yes, how many minutes and rate immediate relief (1-9).
measurement.notes: Mindfulness practice frequency and perceived relief will be used to model short-term downregulation of arousal and urges.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: minutes log1p; relief rating person-mean center

expected_effects.rationale: Reduces momentary worry via downregulating arousal and shifting attention.
Decentering reduces fusion with catastrophic thoughts.
May reduce urge-driven checking in the short term by increasing tolerance.
Improves ability to tolerate urges and resist checking.
Less reactivity reduces distress about loss of control.
Breathing and mindfulness can acutely lower panic intensity.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Low risk; ensure guidance for participants with trauma history (use grounding techniques).

feasibility.notes: High feasibility; short practices easily tracked by EMA.

predictor_variables.label: Executive function / inhibition training (urge pause & response prevention)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Specific practice of inhibitory control and urge delay reduces compulsive checking and failure-to-inhibit behaviors.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Short stop-and-think routines, if-then delay plans, brief computerized inhibitory-control exercises (where available).

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you use an urge-pause/inhibition exercise when you felt the urge to check? (Y/N). If yes, rate success resisting (1-9) and minutes practiced.
measurement.notes: Directly targets C03 and C04; measure perceived success and practice minutes.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: success rating person-mean center; minutes log1p

expected_effects.rationale: By reducing checking, worry maintenance may reduce over hours.
Less compulsive checking weakens reinforcement of catastrophic beliefs.
Direct immediate effect on checking episodes.
Directly trains inhibition; expected strong short-term effects.

Successful inhibition increases perceived control (polarity: higher inhibition practice increases control -> lower C05; sign reported relative to predictor: more P05 -> lower C05 so expected_sign is '-' but we include '+' only if variable denotes failure; to avoid confusion, we use '-' below).
Reducing habitual checking and improving inhibition may reduce panic escalation in some contexts.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Low risk. For computerized inhibitory training, ensure it's brief and optional.

feasibility.notes: Feasible for EMA; effects may manifest at multiple timescales.

predictor_variables.label: Sleep and circadian stabilization
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Better sleep reduces baseline anxiety and arousal reactivity, indirectly reducing worry and panic vulnerability.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Consistent sleep/wake schedule, reduce nocturnal checking, pre-sleep constructive worry planning.

measurement.item_or_signal: Morning diary: last night's sleep onset time, wake time, sleep quality (1-9). Also nightly adherence to sleep routine (1-9). Optional passive: sleep_duration_minutes via wearable / phone (if available).
measurement.notes: Daily morning diary anchors sleep measures; sleep changes will be modeled as a slow-moving predictor (24-72h effects).

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: sleep minutes: z-score per participant; quality: person-mean center

expected_effects.rationale: Improved sleep reduces baseline anxiety across days.
Lower arousal reduces catastrophic appraisals.
Less fatigue may reduce impulsive checking, though indirect.
Better sleep may improve inhibition capacity (operationalized as decreased C04). For clarity, expected_sign here is '-' (more P06 -> lower C04).
Improved sleep supports sense of control and reduces distress.
Sleep effects on panic vulnerability often manifest across nights.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Recommend medical evaluation if serious sleep disorder suspected.

feasibility.notes: Daily diary makes this feasible; passive wearable increases accuracy but is optional.

predictor_variables.label: Social support & accountability mechanisms
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Brief social accountability (check-ins, buddy plans) can reduce avoidance-driven worry and provide alternative feedback to catastrophizing.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Daily check-in with a trusted person or accountability partner about scheduled checking & coping plans.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you reach out to or receive a supportive/check-in message from your accountability partner? (Y/N). Rate perceived helpfulness 1-9 and whether they reminded you of your plan (Y/N).
measurement.notes: Social reminders often moderate urge-driven behaviors; variability occurs across days.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: binary flags as-is; helpfulness person-mean center

expected_effects.rationale: Support reduces immediate worry through reassurance and plan reminders (when adaptive).
External reality checks can help challenge catastrophic appraisals in the moment.
Accountability reduces unplanned checking episodes.
Support can help enact inhibition plans when urges arise.
Regular social support improves perceived control and reduces distress about losing control.
May help reduce panic intensity situationally but effects are context-dependent.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Ensure confidentiality planning; avoid recommending reliance on untrained others for medical advice.

feasibility.notes: Feasible as EMA flags; actual social-contact variability depends on participant network.

edges.notes: Cognitive restructuring expected to reduce catastrophic interpretations within hours to a day.
Scheduled checking and digital hygiene should quickly reduce unplanned checking episodes and duration.
Interoceptive exposure directly targets panic reactivity to bodily cues; effects can be immediate to several days post-session.
Mindfulness/acceptance increases tolerance and decreases failure-to-inhibit checking.
Inhibition/urge-control training directly targets failure to resist checking; strong immediate effects possible.
Sleep stabilization is a slower-moving predictor reducing baseline worry across days.
Social support and accountability can rapidly improve perceived control and reduce distress about loss of control.

expected_collinearity_risks: P01 (cognitive restructuring) and P04 (mindfulness) can correlate—both target appraisal/attention processes; monitor VIF and consider residualizing or combining if very high.
P02 (scheduled checking) and passive screen_time proxies for C03 will be correlated; treat passive minutes and self-report minutes as complementary but watch collinearity.
P05 (inhibition training) overlaps mechanistically with P04 (distress tolerance) but differs in action orientation; moderate risk.
expected_floor_ceiling_risks: C06 (panic intensity) may be zero-inflated if symptoms are infrequent; capture event_flag and consider event-conditioned modeling.
P03 interoceptive exposure minutes may be sparse early, creating floor effects—model as session-level binary + continuous minutes when present.
variance_enhancement_suggestions: Use 1–9 Likert anchored scales to increase variance for subjective constructs.
Collect both duration (minutes) and intensity (1–9) for checking episodes to avoid rare-event binary problems.
Encourage at least brief daily practice of P01/P04 to increase within-person variance in predictors.
excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: Many BIO neuromodulation/pharmacology nodes (TMS, meds) are deprioritized at this behavioral early-intervention stage for feasibility and safety in EMA-only gVAR setup.
Avoid multiple separate CBT-skill variables (e.g., separate reappraisal, metacognitive, and problem-solving) to minimize redundancy—combined into P01.
diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: With 6 criteria + 7 predictors (13 time series) and ~168 momentary observations, an initial gVAR is feasible if adherence is ≥70%. If adherence lower, reduce predictors to top 3–5 (P01, P02, P05, P03 prioritized by likely short-term effects) or use lagged shrinkage priors.

safety_notes: No explicit self-harm or psychosis risk indicated in the complaint. However, intense panic and impairment warrant a recommendation to seek professional evaluation if symptoms include severe functional impairment, syncope, chest pain, or suicidal ideation. Exposure (P03) should be done with safety instructions and stop rules; do not recommend high-intensity interoceptive exposure without clinician oversight. Passive data collection requires informed consent and clear privacy safeguards.
